did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Deep Learning, specifically Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG19, ResNet50, Inception-V3) with transfer learning and fine-tuning, to perform binary classification of skin lesions (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using the ISIC dataset. The results were implemented in an Android application for melanoma screening.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and evaluation of Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG19, ResNet50, Inception-V3) for melanoma screening using the ISIC dataset. It details specific methodology (transfer learning, fine-tuning), reports quantitative results (accuracy, TPR, confusion matrices), and describes the development of a real-time Android application, which constitutes new technical results rather than a summary of prior literature.

title: Melanoma Screening using Deep Neural Networks
journal: 2018 3rd International Conference for Convergence in Technology (I2CT)
brief_summary: This study implements Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG19, ResNet50, and Inception-V3) for the binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and non-melanoma categories using the ISIC dataset. The researchers employed two transfer learning approaches: one without fine-tuning and another with fine-tuning, utilizing a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier on extracted features. The models were evaluated on a test set of 979 images, with performance metrics including accuracy and True Positive Rate (TPR). The combined architecture achieved the highest performance, reaching an accuracy of 0.85 and a TPR of 0.79 with fine-tuning. Additionally, the authors developed a real-time Android application to demonstrate the practical deployment of this deep learning pipeline for melanoma screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for the binary classification task. The paper defines True Positives as 'images of melanoma correctly classified as melanoma' and notes that melanoma is the 'most dangerous form of skin cancer'.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The paper states the dataset was originally divided into 'subtypes of Melanoma, Non-melanoma and remaining being classified as Miscellaneous', but the experiment used a binary scenario of melanoma vs. non-melanoma. The Android app output is described as classifying an image as 'benign which is non-melanoma'.
Mentioned as a category in the original ISIC dataset challenge ('remaining being classified as Miscellaneous'), but explicitly excluded from the binary classification scenario used in this study.

Listed in the Introduction as a type of skin cancer ('Skin cancer is of various types such as Actinic Keratoses , Basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma and melanoma'), but not a specific label used in the experimental classification task.
Listed in the Introduction as a type of skin cancer, but not a specific label used in the experimental classification task.
Listed in the Introduction as a type of skin cancer, but not a specific label used in the experimental classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma or non-melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the work implements a 'binary classification task' to predict whether an input RGB image of a skin lesion is melanoma or not. The models (VGG19, ResNet50, Inception-V3) were used to extract features which were then fed into a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for this binary decision. The output is a discrete class label (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma/Benign), not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC 2016 and 2017 challenges, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The images are in RGB format, mostly 512x768 pixels, in JPEG format. The original dataset included subtypes of Melanoma, Non-melanoma, and Miscellaneous, but this study used a binary classification subset.

use.training: Used to create training, testing, and validation splits for developing the final classifier and evaluating transfer learning and fine-tuning approaches.
use.validation: Created from the dataset to satisfy analysis ratios, though specific count or usage details for validation are not explicitly reported beyond its existence as a split.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the classification metrics (accuracy, TPR, confusion matrices) for VGG19, ResNet50, Inception-V3, and the combined architecture.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: The ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge 2012 dataset, containing one million training images divided into 1,000 categories. Pre-trained weights from this dataset were used for transfer learning.

use.training: Used as the source for pre-trained weights for VGG19, ResNet50, and Inception-V3 architectures.
use.other: Pretraining source for the deep neural networks.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives for each architecture and technique.
To evaluate the overall performance of the classification models.
To measure the ratio of correctly classified melanoma images to the total number of melanoma images in the test data.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, True Positive Rate (TPR), and confusion matrices.

ISIC Dataset (Test Split).description: Evaluation on 979 test images for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma). Results reported for Transfer Learning (TL) and Fine Tuning (FT) techniques across individual architectures and a Combined architecture.
ISIC Dataset (Test Split).notes: The combined architecture with fine-tuning achieved the highest performance (Accuracy: 0.85, TPR: 0.79). The paper notes that the experimental design is sensitive to the choice of lesions for positive and negative classes.

architecture_used.main_model: VGG19, ResNet50, Inception-V3, and a Combined 'Bucket of Models' architecture
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained weights from the ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge 2012 dataset (1 million training images, 1,000 categories)

VGG19.description: Deep Convolutional Neural Network with 19 layers, including additional max-pooling and dropout layers. Convolutional filter kernel size is 3x3.

ResNet50.description: Deep network consisting of convolutional and pooling layers with residual blocks and skipped connections to solve vanishing gradients.

Inception-V3.description: Feature extractor designed at multiple levels, computing 1x1, 3x3, and 5x5 convolutions. Outputs of filters are stacked before being fed to the next layer. Smaller in size than VGG19 and ResNet.

Combined.description: A 'Bucket of Models' approach combining the results of VGG19, ResNet50, and Inception-V3.

training_details.modifications: The last two layers (softmax layers) were removed from the state-of-the-art architectures to use extracted features for a binary SVM classifier.
transfer_learning_steps: Obtain features directly from the last fully connected layer (layer before the removed softmax layer)
fine_tuning_steps: Perform iterations to let the model adapt to the problem
Obtain features in the same way as transfer learning
training_details.svm_classifier: Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier trained on the extracted features

classification_or_output_head.details: A binary SVM classifier was used on the features extracted from the modified CNNs (after removing the last two softmax layers) to classify skin lesions as melanoma or non-melanoma.

preprocessing_techniques: No lesion segmentation or complex higher-level image processing tasks (such as removal of hair, shades, and radiance) were performed; the method is described as free from these steps.
Standard pre-processing steps including cropping and lesion centralization were applied.
Resizing and central cropping of images to 224x224 pixels.
conclusions: The study concludes that Deep Convolutional Neural Networks (VGG19, ResNet50, Inception-V3) combined with transfer learning and fine-tuning are effective for the binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma skin lesions.
The combined 'Bucket of Models' architecture achieved the highest performance, with an accuracy of 0.85 and a True Positive Rate (TPR) of 0.79 on the test set of 979 images.
Fine-tuning the pre-trained networks generally improved performance compared to transfer learning without fine-tuning across the individual architectures.
The proposed method eliminates the need for complex preprocessing steps such as lesion segmentation, hair removal, or shade/radiance removal, thereby saving time and simplifying the pipeline.

The research demonstrates the practical feasibility of deploying deep learning models for melanoma screening through the development of a real-time Android application that processes images in an average of 15 seconds.
The authors acknowledge that the domain of skin cancer research is restricted by the limited availability of open-source, high-quality data in optimum amounts.
The experimental design is noted to be sensitive to the choice of lesions for positive and negative classes, and the results were obtained with a relatively small training set (2900 images).
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the experimental design is sensitive to the choice of lesions for positive and negative classes, potentially due to the relative difficulty of identifying classes.
The training dataset size was relatively small (2900 images), which the authors note as a limitation affecting the generalizability of the results.
The domain of skin cancer research is restricted by the limited availability of open-source, high-quality data in optimum amounts.

The study excluded 'Miscellaneous' lesions from the original ISIC dataset, focusing only on a binary classification of Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma, which may limit clinical applicability to broader dermatological contexts.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; results are reported as descriptive metrics (accuracy, TPR) on a single test split.
The Android application deployment relies on an external server for inference, resulting in an average processing time of 15 seconds, which may impact real-time clinical utility.

The method avoids complex preprocessing steps like lesion segmentation, hair removal, or shade/radiance removal, which simplifies the pipeline but may introduce bias if input images vary significantly in quality or composition.
The dataset used was a mix of ISIC 2016 and 2017 challenges, but specific details on class balance or demographic distribution within the training/test splits are not provided.
